title: Wholesale Deal: 3BR Ranch in Atlanta, GA
howFound: Driving for dollars — vacant property with overgrown lawn
keyLesson: The seller was an out-of-state heir who just wanted to unload the property quickly. Speed and certainty mattered more than price.
timeline: Day 1: Found property while driving for dollars
Day 3: Skip traced owner, made contact
Day 5: Visited property, made offer at $125K
Day 7: Contract signed
Day 10: Found cash buyer at $137K
Day 14: Assignment closed, collected $12K fee

title: Wholesale Deal: 4BR Brick Home in Houston, TX
howFound: Probate lead list — contacted personal representative of estate
keyLesson: Probate leads take patience. It took 6 follow-ups over 3 months before the PR was ready to sell. Consistent follow-up wins.
timeline: Month 1: Mailed probate lead, no response
Month 2: Second mailer and call, brief conversation
Month 3: PR ready to sell, scheduled walkthrough
Day 1: Inspected property, offered $98K
Day 5: Contract executed
Day 14: Marketed to buyer's list, accepted $116.5K
Day 21: Closed assignment, collected $18.5K

title: Wholesale Deal: 2BR Condo in Phoenix, AZ
howFound: Cold calling absentee owners from tax records
keyLesson: Even smaller assignment fees add up fast. This deal took minimal effort because the seller was highly motivated — behind on HOA dues and facing liens.
timeline: Day 1: Cold called absentee owner list
Day 2: Owner expressed interest in selling
Day 4: Virtual walkthrough, made offer at $115K
Day 5: Contract signed
Day 8: Posted in local investor Facebook groups
Day 9: Buyer committed at $123.5K
Day 11: Assignment closed, collected $8.5K

title: Wholesale Deal: 3BR Bungalow in Detroit, MI
howFound: Tax delinquent list — owner owed 3 years of back taxes
keyLesson: Low-price markets mean lower fees per deal, but the volume is incredible. I closed 4 Detroit deals that same month for a combined $22K.
timeline: Day 1: Pulled tax delinquent list from county
Day 2: Direct mail batch sent
Day 4: Owner called back, desperate to avoid tax auction
Day 5: Walked property, offered $18K
Day 6: Contract signed
Day 7: Sent to cash buyer list
Day 9: Assigned at $23.5K, collected $5.5K

title: Wholesale Deal: 4BR Split-Level in Jacksonville, FL
howFound: PPC Google Ads — seller searched 'sell my house fast Jacksonville'
keyLesson: Paid marketing works when your cost per lead stays under $500. This lead cost $320 and returned $22K. The ROI on targeted PPC is unmatched for motivated sellers.
timeline: Day 1: Seller submitted form from Google Ad
Day 2: Called seller, scheduled appointment
Day 4: Inspected property, offered $185K
Day 6: Negotiated to $185K, contract signed
Day 10: Listed for assignment at $207K
Day 14: Buyer under contract
Day 18: Assignment closed, collected $22K

title: Wholesale Deal: 3BR Ranch in Memphis, TN
howFound: Bandit signs — seller called sign posted near property
keyLesson: Old-school marketing still works. Bandit signs cost almost nothing and generate calls from truly motivated sellers who want simplicity over top dollar.
timeline: Day 1: Placed 20 bandit signs in target neighborhood
Day 3: Received call from elderly homeowner wanting to downsize
Day 4: Inspected property, offered $52K
Day 5: Contract signed
Day 8: Marketed to buyer list, 3 interested buyers
Day 10: Best buyer at $61K
Day 12: Assignment closed, collected $9K

title: Wholesale Deal: Duplex in Cleveland, OH
howFound: Code violation list from city building department
keyLesson: Duplexes attract rental investors who will pay more because they see future cash flow. Marketing multi-units to landlords gets faster buyer commitments.
timeline: Day 1: Pulled code violation list from city records
Day 3: Contacted owner via skip trace
Day 5: Owner agreed to sell to avoid fines
Day 7: Contract signed at $45K
Day 10: Marketed to landlord buyer list
Day 13: Buyer committed at $52.5K
Day 16: Assignment closed, collected $7.5K

title: Wholesale Deal: 3BR Adobe in San Antonio, TX
howFound: Expired listing on MLS — agent relationship referral
keyLesson: Building relationships with listing agents who handle expired listings creates a pipeline of warm leads. The agent gets a referral bonus, you get the deal.
timeline: Day 1: Agent referred expired listing seller
Day 3: Met with seller, discussed options
Day 5: Offered $92K cash, no contingencies
Day 7: Contract signed
Day 12: Marketed to investor network
Day 15: Buyer at $107K
Day 19: Assignment closed, collected $15K

title: Wholesale Deal: 4BR Colonial in Indianapolis, IN
howFound: Direct mail to pre-foreclosure list
keyLesson: Pre-foreclosure sellers have a deadline. That urgency means they make decisions faster. But you must approach with empathy — they are going through a tough time.
timeline: Day 1: Sent yellow letters to pre-foreclosure list
Day 5: Homeowner called, facing auction in 45 days
Day 7: Inspected property, offered $78K
Day 9: Contract signed
Day 12: Marketed to cash buyer network
Day 16: Buyer locked in at $92K
Day 20: Assignment closed, collected $14K

title: Wholesale Deal: 3BR Townhome in Charlotte, NC
howFound: Networking at local REIA meeting — investor wanted out of a bad tenant situation
keyLesson: Not all wholesale deals come from distressed homeowners. Burned-out landlords are some of the best leads. They understand investor pricing and just want out.
timeline: Day 1: Met tired landlord at Charlotte REIA meeting
Day 3: Toured property with tenant in place
Day 5: Offered $195K, seller countered at $200K
Day 7: Settled at $195K, contract signed
Day 10: Marketed to turnkey rental investors
Day 13: Buyer committed at $225K
Day 17: Assignment closed, collected $30K

title: Flip Deal: 4BR Craftsman in Dallas, TX
howFound: Driving for dollars — boarded-up windows in rapidly gentrifying neighborhood
keyLesson: Location trumps condition. This house was rough, but the neighborhood was on fire with new development. Comparable homes were selling fast above list price.
timeline: Month 1: Found property, negotiated purchase at $180K
Month 1: Closed with hard money, began demo
Month 2: Full gut renovation — new kitchen, baths, HVAC, roof
Month 3: Finished major systems, started cosmetics
Month 4: Staging and professional photography
Month 4: Listed at $375K
Month 5: Accepted offer at $365K, closed in 30 days

title: Flip Deal: 3BR Block Home in Tampa, FL
financingUsed: Hard money loan at 11% — 85% of purchase + 100% rehab
howFound: Direct mail to absentee owner — property was rented but severely neglected
keyLesson: Scope creep kills flip profits. We discovered foundation issues during demo that added $12K. Always budget 15-20% contingency on older Florida block homes.
timeline: Month 1: Purchased at $155K, tenant vacated
Month 1-2: Demo revealed foundation crack, added $12K to budget
Month 2-3: Full interior renovation — open concept, new kitchen, luxury vinyl
Month 3: Exterior paint, landscaping, new front door
Month 4: Listed at $319K, received 4 offers in first weekend
Month 4: Accepted $310K, closed in 3 weeks

title: Flip Deal: 2BR Cottage in Nashville, TN
financingUsed: Private money lender at 10% — interest only
howFound: Off-market deal from another wholesaler
keyLesson: Even a 2BR can command premium prices in the right neighborhood. We added a bathroom and converted the attic to a loft, which buyers loved. Nashville's East Side is gold.
timeline: Month 1: Purchased from wholesaler at $210K
Month 1: Permits pulled for bathroom addition and attic conversion
Month 2-3: Added bathroom, converted attic to usable loft space
Month 3-4: Full kitchen renovation, refinished original hardwoods
Month 5: Staged with mid-century modern furniture
Month 5: Listed at $399K
Month 6: Sold at $395K after minor negotiation

title: Flip Deal: 3BR Bi-Level in Denver, CO
financingUsed: Hard money at 12.5% — cross-collateralized with another property
howFound: Driving for dollars — house had 3 code violation notices taped to the door
keyLesson: Denver's market rewards quality finishes. We went with quartz counters, tile showers, and smart home features. Higher rehab cost but the ARV jumped $30K above initial estimate.
timeline: Month 1: Contacted owner via skip trace, negotiated $320K
Month 1: Closed with hard money, demo started
Month 2: Structural repairs, new HVAC, electrical panel upgrade
Month 3: Kitchen and bath renovations with premium finishes
Month 4: Exterior updates, smart thermostat, Ring doorbell, landscaping
Month 4: Listed at $535K
Month 5: Multiple offers, accepted $525K

title: Flip Deal: 3BR Brick Ranch in Birmingham, AL
financingUsed: Cash purchase — used HELOC from primary residence
howFound: Auction.com — bank-owned REO property
keyLesson: REO properties from online auctions can be great deals if you do your homework before bidding. We inspected the exterior and ran comps beforehand. No surprises.
timeline: Week 1: Won auction at $58K, completed purchase
Month 1: Cleared out debris, assessed full scope
Month 2: New roof, HVAC, updated electrical
Month 3: Kitchen, baths, flooring, paint
Month 3: Listed at $159K
Month 4: Sold at $155K to FHA buyer

title: Flip Deal: 4BR Victorian in Columbus, OH
financingUsed: Hard money at 13% + private money second for rehab
howFound: Probate lead — heirs lived out of state and wanted a quick sale
keyLesson: Victorian-era homes have hidden costs — lead paint, knob-and-tube wiring, plaster walls. Budget generously. The final ARV exceeded expectations because buyers pay a premium for character.
timeline: Month 1: Purchased from estate at $135K
Month 1-2: Lead abatement, rewiring, plumbing updates
Month 3-4: Restored original woodwork, added modern kitchen
Month 5: Master bath renovation, new HVAC
Month 6: Exterior restoration, period-appropriate paint colors
Month 6: Listed at $325K
Month 7: Sold at $315K

title: Flip Deal: 3BR Ranch in Raleigh, NC
howFound: Wholesaler assignment — paid $8K assignment fee
keyLesson: Buying from wholesalers is a legitimate strategy. You pay a premium but save time on marketing and lead generation. The numbers still worked beautifully here.
timeline: Week 1: Purchased from wholesaler at $215K (included $8K assignment)
Month 1: Permitted for kitchen expansion into dining room
Month 2: Open concept conversion, new kitchen with island
Month 3: Both baths renovated, new flooring throughout
Month 3: Listed at $389K
Month 4: Bidding war, sold at $385K

title: Flip Deal: 5BR Foursquare in St. Louis, MO
howFound: Vacant property list from city — owner tracked via county records
keyLesson: Large homes in transitional neighborhoods can yield outsized returns. The key is timing the market correctly — this area had 3 new restaurants and a brewery open within a year.
timeline: Month 1: Purchased vacant property at $85K cash
Month 1-2: Full gut — new plumbing, electrical, HVAC
Month 3-4: Framed new bathroom, rebuilt kitchen, restored staircase
Month 5: Finished flooring, paint, fixtures, landscaping
Month 5: Listed at $255K
Month 6: Sold at $245K

title: BRRRR Deal: 3BR Ranch in Kansas City, MO
financingUsed: Hard money for purchase + rehab, refinanced into 30-year DSCR loan
howFound: Driving for dollars — boarded windows, mail piling up
keyLesson: The BRRRR strategy lets you recycle capital indefinitely. We pulled out 92% of our cash and kept a property that cash flows $380/month. Infinite return.
timeline: Month 1: Purchased at $62K with hard money
Month 1-3: Full renovation — kitchen, bath, roof, HVAC
Month 4: Tenant placed at $1,250/month
Month 6: Seasoning period for refinance
Month 8: Appraised at $148K, refinanced at 75% LTV ($111K)
Month 8: Paid off hard money ($97K total), pocketed $14K cash back

title: BRRRR Deal: Duplex in Milwaukee, WI
financingUsed: Private money at 10%, refinanced into conventional 30-year
howFound: Tax delinquent list — owner owed $8K in back taxes
keyLesson: Duplexes are BRRRR gold. Two units mean higher rental income, better DSCR ratios for refinancing, and more cash flow. This property cash flows $620/month after the refi.
timeline: Month 1: Purchased at $55K, paid off seller's tax liens as part of deal
Month 1-3: Renovated both units — new kitchens, baths, flooring
Month 4: Unit 1 rented at $850/month
Month 4: Unit 2 rented at $825/month
Month 6: Seasoning period begins
Month 9: Appraised at $168K, refinanced at 75% LTV ($126K)
Month 9: Paid off private lender ($103K), recovered $23K cash

title: BRRRR Deal: 3BR Row House in Pittsburgh, PA
financingUsed: Cash purchase, refinanced into DSCR loan at 7.75%
howFound: Wholesaler — paid $5K assignment fee built into purchase price
keyLesson: Pittsburgh row houses in neighborhoods near hospitals and universities have insatiable rental demand. Our tenant waitlist had 15 applicants within a week of listing.
timeline: Month 1: Purchased at $48K cash from wholesaler
Month 1-3: Full interior renovation — modernized layout, added half bath
Month 4: Rented at $1,150/month to travel nurse
Month 7: Began refinance process
Month 10: Appraised at $138K, refinanced at 75% LTV ($103.5K)
Month 10: Recovered $103.5K — original investment was $90K, pocketed $13.5K

title: BRRRR Deal: 4BR Colonial in Richmond, VA
financingUsed: Hard money at 11.5%, refinanced into 30-year conventional
howFound: Direct mail to high-equity absentee owners
keyLesson: Richmond's Church Hill neighborhood is a BRRRR paradise. Strong appreciation plus solid rental demand. The appraisal came in $10K above our ARV estimate.
timeline: Month 1: Purchased at $110K with hard money loan
Month 1-3: Major renovation — new roof, updated systems, modern kitchen
Month 4: Added central AC (house had radiator heat only)
Month 5: Rented at $1,650/month
Month 7: Started refinance application
Month 9: Appraised at $255K, refinanced at 75% LTV ($191.25K)
Month 9: Paid off hard money ($175K total), pocketed $16.25K

title: BRRRR Deal: 3BR Bungalow in Oklahoma City, OK
financingUsed: Private money at 9%, refinanced into DSCR loan
howFound: Driving for dollars — noticed new roof but abandoned interior through window
keyLesson: OKC has some of the best rent-to-price ratios in the country. This property hits the 1% rule easily and the DSCR loan was approved without any issues.
timeline: Month 1: Purchased at $72K with private money
Month 1-2: Interior gut renovation — kitchen, baths, flooring
Month 3: Updated landscaping and exterior paint
Month 4: Rented at $1,200/month
Month 6: Seasoning period
Month 8: Appraised at $158K, refinanced at 75% LTV ($118.5K)
Month 8: Paid off private lender ($110K), pocketed $8.5K

title: BRRRR Deal: 4BR Split-Level in Little Rock, AR
financingUsed: Hard money at 12%, refinanced into 30-year DSCR loan at 8%
howFound: Pre-foreclosure list — owner 4 months behind on mortgage
keyLesson: Helping a homeowner avoid foreclosure while building your portfolio is a win-win. The seller walked away with $8K after paying off their mortgage, which was better than a foreclosure on their record.
timeline: Month 1: Contacted pre-foreclosure owner, negotiated $65K
Month 1: Closed with hard money, began renovation
Month 2-3: New HVAC, updated kitchen, refinished hardwoods
Month 4: Both bathrooms renovated, exterior paint
Month 5: Rented at $1,350/month
Month 8: Started refinance
Month 10: Appraised at $162K, refinanced at 75% LTV ($121.5K)
Month 10: Paid off hard money ($110K), recovered $11.5K

title: Buy & Hold: Duplex in Baltimore, MD
howFound: MLS listing — sat for 60 days, seller dropped price twice
keyLesson: Not every deal needs to be off-market. This was an on-market deal that most investors ignored because it needed cosmetic work. Light rehab plus strong rents equals great cash flow.
timeline: Week 1: Found on MLS after 60 days on market, offered $95K (listed at $115K)
Week 2: Seller countered at $100K, we held firm at $95K
Week 3: Seller accepted $95K
Month 1: Closed, began light cosmetic rehab on both units
Month 2: Unit 1 rented at $950/month
Month 2: Unit 2 rented at $925/month
Ongoing: Property managed by local PM company at 8%

title: Buy & Hold: 3BR Brick Ranch in Cincinnati, OH
howFound: Wholesaler deal — assignment fee was $6K
keyLesson: Section 8 tenants provide guaranteed rent payments from the government. This property was approved for $1,250/month Section 8 voucher — above market rent for the area.
timeline: Week 1: Purchased from wholesaler at $82K
Month 1: Light rehab — paint, flooring, appliances, landscaping
Month 2: Applied for Section 8 inspection
Month 2: Passed inspection, listed on Section 8 portal
Month 3: Tenant placed at $1,250/month (Section 8 voucher)
Ongoing: Stable cash flow with government-backed rent

title: Buy & Hold: 4BR Home in Tulsa, OK
howFound: Off-market deal from property manager who knew owner wanted to sell
keyLesson: Building relationships with property managers creates a deal pipeline most investors never access. PMs know which owners are burned out before anyone else.
timeline: Week 1: PM connected us with tired landlord
Week 2: Negotiated $72K purchase price
Week 3: Closed, PM already had tenant lined up
Month 1: Light rehab while unit was occupied (exterior, systems)
Month 2: Tenant transitioned to new lease at $1,100/month
Ongoing: Same PM manages property, seamless transition

title: Subject-To Deal: 3BR Home in Orlando, FL
financingUsed: Subject-to existing mortgage — seller's 3.25% FHA loan assumed informally
howFound: Facebook Marketplace — seller posted 'need to sell ASAP, relocating for work'
keyLesson: Subject-to deals let you acquire properties with incredible financing terms that no longer exist in today's market. This seller's 3.25% rate on a $248K balance is worth more than the equity spread.
timeline: Day 1: Found seller's Facebook post, made contact
Day 3: Met seller, learned they had 3.25% FHA loan with $248K balance
Day 5: Offered to take over payments subject-to existing mortgage
Day 7: Attorney reviewed, contracts signed, deed transferred
Day 10: Seller relocated, we took over $1,580/month payment
Month 1: Light cosmetic updates — paint, landscaping
Month 2: Rented at $2,350/month

title: Seller Financing Deal: Fourplex in San Antonio, TX
financingUsed: Seller financing — $30K down, 5.5% interest, 20-year amortization, 5-year balloon
howFound: Direct mail to free-and-clear multifamily owners over 65
keyLesson: Retired owners who own properties free and clear often prefer monthly income over a lump sum. Seller financing gives them passive income and gives you leverage with great terms.
timeline: Month 1: Mailed free-and-clear multifamily owners
Month 1: Owner responded — owned property 30 years, wanted income not headaches
Month 2: Negotiated $280K with $30K down, 5.5%, 20-year am, 5-year balloon
Month 2: Closed with attorney, deed transferred
Month 3: Light rehab on 2 vacant units
Month 4: All 4 units rented — $875, $850, $850, $825/month
Ongoing: $3,400/month gross rent against $1,830/month seller finance payment

title: Subject-To Wrap Deal: 4BR Home in Austin, TX
financingUsed: Subject-to existing VA loan at 2.75%, wrapped with seller financing to new buyer at 6.5%
howFound: Referral from real estate attorney — divorcing couple needed quick resolution
keyLesson: A subject-to wrap lets you profit from the rate spread without being a landlord. The underlying 2.75% VA loan stays in place while the end buyer pays 6.5%. The $485/month spread is pure profit with no property management.
timeline: Week 1: Attorney referred divorcing couple, both wanted out of mortgage
Week 2: Took property subject-to their 2.75% VA loan ($365K balance)
Week 2: Existing payment was $1,890/month
Week 3: Found credit-worthy buyer who couldn't qualify for traditional financing
Week 4: Sold on wrap at $465K — $25K down, 6.5% on $440K, $2,375/month
Ongoing: Collect $2,375, pay $1,890, net $485/month spread
